content.problemStatement:
The median of an array BBB of length KKK is obtained as follows:


Sort BBB in non-decreasing order, i.e. B1≤B2≤…≤BKB_1 \le B_2 \le \ldots \le B_KB1​≤B2​≤…≤BK​.
After sorting, the median is the element at index ⌈K2⌉\left\lceil \frac{K}{2} \right\rceil⌈2K​⌉ of BBB.

In other words, after sorting, if KKK is odd, the median is the middle element, and if KKK is even the median is the left of the two middle elements.
For an array AAA of length NNN, we define f(A)f(A)f(A) to be the set of integers that appear as the median of at least one contiguous subarray of AAA **that has length greater than 111.**

That is,

f(A)={median(A[L,R])∣1≤L<R≤N}f(A) = \{\text{median}(A[L, R]) \mid 1 \le L \lt R \le N \}f(A)={median(A[L,R])∣1≤L<R≤N}where A[L,R]A[L, R]A[L,R] denotes the subarray [AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​].

You are given a permutation†^\dagger† PPP of the integers {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N}.

Let P[1,i]P[1, i]P[1,i] denote the subarray [P1,P2,…,Pi][P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_i][P1​,P2​,…,Pi​], i.e. the prefix of length iii of PPP.

Your task is to report the value of f(P[1,i])f(P[1, i])f(P[1,i]) for each iii.

That is, for each prefix of PPP, you must report the number of distinct elements that appear as the median of at least one subarray with length >1\gt 1>1.

†^\dagger† A permutation of the integers {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N} is an array that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once each.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line contains a single integer NNN, the length of the permutation.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,…,PNP_1, \ldots, P_NP1​,…,PN​, denoting the elements of the permutation.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line NNN space-separated integers. The iii-th integer should be the value of f(P[1,i])f(P[1, i])f(P[1,i]).
constraints: PPP is a permutation of {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N}.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We have the following:

P[1,1]=[1]P[1, 1] = [1]P[1,1]=[1], there are no medians among subarrays with length >1\gt 1>1.
P[1,2]=[1,2]P[1, 2] = [1, 2]P[1,2]=[1,2], the only median is the median of [1,2][1, 2][1,2] itself, which is 111.

Test case 222: We have the following:

P[1,1]=[2]P[1, 1] = [2]P[1,1]=[2], there are no medians among subarrays with length >1\gt 1>1.
P[1,2]=[2,1]P[1, 2] = [2, 1]P[1,2]=[2,1], the only median is the median of [2,1][2, 1][2,1] itself, which is 111.
P[1,3]=[2,1,3]P[1, 3] = [2, 1, 3]P[1,3]=[2,1,3], now both 111 and 222 appear as medians of some subarray with length >1\gt 1>1; so the count is 222.
